We recommended to the management, the following: 

1. Require the concerned Accountable Officers to liquidate immediately their cash advances and demand refunds of the unused balances, otherwise, cause the withholding of their salaries and wages to compel them to settle their account; (Closed in Part III of the CY 2023 AAR) 

2. Request for write-off of long outstanding cash advances whose creditors are no longer located/deceased, if warranted; and 

3. Enforce strict compliance to the provisions on the grant, utilization and liquidation of cash advances more specifically avoid granting additional cash advances to employees with outstanding unliquidated cash advances. (Closed in Part III of the CY 2023 AAR)

7.7 We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Accounting Office to discontinue the practice of closing year-end receivables for uncollected RPT and SET. In compliance with the Modified Accrual method and IPSAS 23, all prior year receivables should remain recorded, and new receivables for the current year should be established through additional journal entries based on the certified list of collectible accounts from the Treasurer.
7.8 We further recommended that the Accounting Office revert the previous entries by drawing up the necessary Journal Entry Voucher (JEV) to recognize and reflect the total outstanding receivables totaling P14,952,540.27 from prior years, based on the records of the Municipal Treasurer’s Office (MTO).

11.9 We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Accounting Office and the Municipal Budget Office to submit a detailed breakdown of the adjustments reflected in the reconciliation between the actual amounts presented in the Statement of Comparison of Budget and Actual Amounts (SCBAA) for the year ended December 31, 2024, for verification. A justification for the noted deficiencies should likewise be submitted.
11.10 Henceforth, management should ensure that future submissions of the SCBAA and its corresponding reconciliation include detailed disclosures and a breakdown of material items in the Notes to the Financial Statements to enhance the fair presentation and transparency of the financial reports.

12.7 We recommended that management refrain from recording purchases under “Fuel, Oil, and Lubricants Inventory” when the Direct Retail Purchase mode is used. This account is only appropriate for stored fuel, not for items meant for immediate use.

13.9 We recommended the Municipal Mayor to require the establishment and implementation of adequate control mechanisms to ensure that transactions reviewed and approved by the Municipal Accountant are accurately and consistently posted into the accounting system.
13.10 Furthermore, the accounting system being used should have distinct prepare and approve functions to allow segregation of duties and ensure that all entries inputted into the system are subject to electronic review and approval by the Municipal Accountant. This will help validate the accuracy of postings, detect discrepancies early, and ensure accountability throughout the encoding and posting process.

18.10 We recommended the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accounting Office, in coordination with the Municipal Treasurer’s Office and the General Services Office (if established), to ensure strict compliance with Sections 114 and 120 of the NGAS Manual Volume I by:
Maintaining inventory subsidiary ledger cards per inventory type using the perpetual inventory method to provide accurate and up-to-date inventory balances;
Utilizing the ECPAC system utilities to optimize and automate the maintenance of subsidiary ledger cards, thereby improving accuracy, efficiency, and real-time tracking of inventories; and
Addressing manpower constraints through capacity-building measures, reallocation of duties, or additional staffing as necessary to support the proper implementation of inventory control procedures.

19.9 We recommended the Municipal Mayor to require the Municipal Accountant and OIC-Municipal Health Officer in coordination of former OIC-MHO to submit the required supporting documents to facilitate the review of the herein transactions.

We recommended to the Municipal Mayor to require the MACCO to ensure compliance to IPSAS 3 for transactions related to prior period errors.
Henceforth, when practicable, restate the opening balances of the related net assets/equity balances or comparative amounts related to prior period errors, whichever is appropriate,
Provide the necessary disclosures to ensure that the financial statements are fairly presented; and
Moreover, observe correct presentation in the Statement of Changes in Net Assets/Equity.

Management Comment:
Management agreed to the recommendation.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Treasurers Office to take charge in the inventory keeping, or considering that the LGU is a 1st class Municipality, install a General Services Office complete with personnel to establish proper accountability of the LGU properties.
Require the MMO personnel, the MTO, and the MAccO to submit a status report on the delivery of the undelivered goods, unrecorded fuel issuances, and inventory that is yet to be accounted.
Moreover, require the MMO personnel together with the MTO to submit the Delivery/Charge Invoices pertaining the payments enumerated in Annex F hereof which were not substantiated with the same, otherwise, the said transaction shall be disallowed in audit. Henceforth, ensure that payments are only made for delivered goods.
In addition, require the BAC to ensure that the delivery terms/schedule is responsive to the storage capacity of the LGU.
Management Comment:
Managament commented, as represented by the Municipal Accounting Office, that as of April 2024, an amount of P6,070,360.20 related to prior years’ inventory was already posted as liquidation. In addition, liquidations totaling P2,544,295.40 was submitted to them but is yet to be recorded as the same is still subject for review.
Auditors Rejoinder:
The fact thar there are issuances allegedly pertaining to CY 2023 inventory that were consumed in CY 2023 that has not been recorded on the said year highlights managements deficiency on the reporting of its consumed inventories.
Moreover, the management comment is insufficient in addressing the audit issue and recommendation.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor to:
Require the implementation of the established inventory process per Chapter 7 of Volume II of NGAS Manual. Empower the MTO with regards to inventory custodianship, issuance, and reporting.
Moreover, relative to the 35 withdrawal slips, require the MTO/GSO, MAccO, and MEO to collaborate to determine the cost of the inventories issued to expedite posting of the issued inventory in the books of accounts of the LGU.
Relative to the beginning balances, require the same offices to collect and determine the appropriate withdrawal slips on the prior year to facilitate recording in the books.
Management Comment:
Management commented, as represented by the Muncipal Accounting Offie, that the recommendations could not be implemented due to the absence of an established GSO and absence of proper storage area. It said that it is only maximizing the available data provided.
Auditors Rejoinder:
It should be emphasized that in the CY 2022 AAR, the establishment of a proper inventory system  and General Services Office were recommended given that the LGU is a 1st class municipality.
Nevertheless, the other offices involved in the herein deficiency did not sufficiently address the issue at hand both in writing and in the exit conference and no commitments were made to address the same.

We recommended to the Municipal Mayor to:
Require the Municipal Accountant to determine the acquisition dates of the PPE sub accounts herein determined to facilitate computation of depreciation for CY 2023 and prior years.
Prepare appropriate adjusting entry taking into consideration the proper treatment of prior period adjustments.
Henceforth, ensure proper turn-over is conducted in times of manpower movement.

We recommended to the Municipal Mayor to:
Submit a justification for the non-implementation of the one-time cleansing;
In addition, require the Property and Accounting Offices to expedite and prioritize the conduct of one-time cleansing together with the Inventory Committee in line with the guidelines provided in COA Circular No. 2020-006 dated January 31, 2020 to ascertain that assets recorded exists and are actually in the control of the LGU; and
Prepare the necessary adjusting entry.

We recommended to the Municipal Mayor to:
Require the Municipal Accountanting Office to ensure compliance to IPSAS 23 relative to the recognition of transactions from revenue from non-exchange transaction.
Observe proper restatement of CY 2023 accounts and financial statements in the submission of the CY 2024 comparative financial statements in the submission of the CY 2024 comparative financial statements.
Management Comment:
Management agreed to the recommendation.

We recommended to the Head of the Agency to require the MAccO to:
Correct the PPE (Construction in Progress – Infrastructure Assets) and Government Equity account by drawing the appropriate correcting entry, taking into account the treatment of errors per IPSAS 3;
Moreover, ensure appropriate disclosures and retrospective application is made relative thereto; and
Submit a copy of the JEV containing the correction for our review.
Management Comment:
Management agreed to the recommendation and a JEV was already drawn for the adjustment.

We recommended the Municipal Mayor to require the MAccO to prepare the necessary adjusting entry to correct the Government Equity and Inventory Accounts of the LGU.
Management Comment:
Management agreed to the recommendation and made a correcting entry in CY 2024.

We recommended the Municipal Mayor to require the MAccO to prepare the necessary adjusting entry to correct the Government Equity and Inventory Accounts of the LGU. Observe proper presentation and disclosure in the Notes to Financial Statement in the ensuing year for entries covering prior period error.
Management Comment:
Management agreed to the recommendation and made a correcting entry in CY 2024.

We recommended to the Municipal Mayor, to require the accountable officials and employees to:
Liquidate and/or refund the amount of the cash advance ;
(2) Submit a valid justification for the delayed and/or non-liquidation thereof within the period prescribed, stating explicitly the reason for the delay, otherwise, administrative liability and penalty shall be filed; and
(3) File administrative charges against erring officials and criminal charges, if warranted.
Management Comment:
Management commented on its reply that as of March 2024, only P149,605.76 is left unliquidated, broken down as follows:
Moreover, the Municipal Accounting Office stated that demand letters has already been sent to the Accountable Officers involved.

While there has been a significant decrease in the unliquidated cash advance, a valid justification for the delayed and/or non-liquidation thereof were not submitted by the Accountable Officials involved for assessment to facilitate appropriate action.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the MEO to submit the supporting documents required as enumerated in the foregoing paragraphs to validly substantiate the existence and validity of time extension allegedly granted by the LGU to the contractors. Otherwise, recompute the liquidated damages and submit the same to COA with supporting documents and ensure enforcement and collection thereof from the contractors.

Management commented, as represented by the Muncipal Accounting Office, that it trusted that the documents were duly reviewed and attached by the BAC, thus, it acted in good faith in the processing of the transactions.
The Muncipal Engineering Office committed to submit the documents required.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the MEO to:
Submit a valid justification for failure to submit the documents necessary for evaluation, otherwise, subject cost of the components of the variation order shall be issued with appropriate Notices of Suspension and/or Disallowance; and
Submit the documents necessary, as enumerated herein, to facilitate legal and technical review thereof.
Management Comment:
The Management, as represented by the Municipal Engineering Office, committed to submit the documents required relative to the variation order.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor to:
Require the Municipal Engineering Office to submit the lacking documentary requirements, as noted in Annex L hereof.
Henceforth, require the same office to ensure that the Statement of Time Elapsed is prepared in frequencies deemed appropriate.
Require the MAccO to ensure completeness of the documentary requirements as required by the existing laws, rules, and regulations.

Management commented, as represented by the Muncipal Accounting Office that it acted in good faith in processing the transaction as it trusted that the said documents were already attached. It also added that the staff assigned in pre-audit was new at that time and not familiar with all the documents that needed to be checked.
Nonetheless, it admitted that there were times wherein payments were pushed thru despite incomplete supporting documents due to accommodation of the request by the facilitating office/personnel, in exigency of public service.
It vowed to strictly implement the non-processing of financial transactions with incomplete supporting documents.

We recommended to the Municipal Mayor to require the BAC to review and improve its procurement processes, ensure compliance with legal and regulatory requirements, enhance documentation by ensuring that the proper Contract Agreement Form is utilized for procurements via public bidding instead of Purchase Order.
Management Comment:
The Management agreed to the recommendation.

We recommended the Municipal Mayor to require the Municipal Treasurer, the Municipal Accountant, and the DO’s/SDOs concerned to be updated with existing tax laws, rules and regulations and ensure compliance thereof, such as the withholding of taxes from the payments made to suppliers.
Moreover, discontinue the practice of procurement thru cash advance.
Management Comment:
The management agreed to the recommendation.

We recommended to the Municipal Mayor to require the MTO to submit the disbursement vouchers related to the herein enumerated checks for audit. 






Henceforth, ensure that no disbursement shall be made for request for payments with lacking valid documents. 





Also, require the MACCO to ensure timely preparation of the monthly Bank Reconciliation Statements, it being an essential internal control tool and may prevent and/or detect fraud

observations.recommendation:
We recommended to the Municipal Treasurer the following:
Trace back the composition of the time deposit in the Trust Fund and search for its Memorandum of Agreement (MOA). Review the provisions stipulated in the Memorandum of Agreement.
Close the Time Deposit account straightaway and refrain from opening Time Deposit using the Trust Fund. Moreover, to coordinate with the Municipal Planning and Development Coordinator (MPDC) and Municipal Engineer with the immediate implementation of the Trust Fund project. Management must insure that the fund is used at its optimum in order to attain its specific purpose and so that the constituents can savor the benefits that can be derived therefrom in due time.

We recommended to the concerned officials and employee to fully liquidate their outstanding cash advances which accumulated to ₱9,754,451.96 immediately and submit the required liquidation reports to the Municipal Accountant for review and recording in the General Fund books of accounts before forwarding them to COA for verification.
Further, we recommended to the management to strictly control the grant of cash advances and insure that previous cash advances are fully liquidated first before granting a new one. Also, the officials and employees must submit a liquidation report immediately once the purpose has been served.

We recommended to the Municipal Mayor to immediately discontinue the drawing of special cash advances personally and regularly except for official travelling expenses and intelligence and confidential expenses.
Moreover, Purchase Requests and Activity Designs must be properly filled up. The unit cost and total cost per line item must be supplied. In a similar way, the number of participants and the participants must be indicated in the Activity Design at all times. These are necessary in order to properly determine the reasonableness and fairness of the appropriation per activity and to prevent excessive and extravagant disbursements of government funds.
Additionally, the Municipal Mayor must immediately designate a Special Disbursing Officer (SDO) to draw, disburse and record in the cashbook impending special cash advances of the LGU per Section 6 of COA Circular No. 97-002 subject to cash examination by COA. Regular reconciliation of cash advance account must also be implemented between the cashbook of the Special Disbursing Officer and the subsidiary ledger of the Municipal Accounting Office in order to strengthen the internal control and in order to ascertain that check and balance of account does exist in the LGU. Also, the Municipal Accountant must record the special cash advances in the Advances to Special Disbursing Officer rather than Advances to Operating Expenses for proper accounting and for easy verification by the auditor during cash examination and audit sessions.

We recommended to the Property Officer to issue Inventory Custodian Slips (ICSs) immediately to custodians of the aforementioned assets to establish accountability and to protect them from possible loss and misuse.
In addition, the Municipal Accountant should record inventories held for consumption to their appropriate inventory accounts upon purchase/acquisition, and record them to their expense counterparts only when adequate proof of their issuance, transfer or other disposal is already established pursuant to Section 114 of the Manual on NGAS Volume 1 and COA Circular 2005-2.

We recommended to management the following courses of action:
The Municipal Accountant must coordinate with the Municipal Engineer in gathering the required documentary supports such as Certificates of Project Completion and Turn-over or 100% Statements of Work Accomplished (SWA), Certificate of Acceptance, Inspection Reports, Deeds of Donation and the like of the ₱ 143,386,309.87 adjusting entry and submit them to COA immediately for further review and validation by the auditor.
The Municipal Accountant must initially record and account as assets the projects and equipment that are externally funded in the Trust Fund books in order to prevent loss and unaccounted assets. More so, in order for the LGU to project an accurate, reliable and fairly presented Trust Fund financial statements.
The Municipal Accountant must always support every adjusting entry with proper and complete documentary requirements in order to establish its correctness, validity and legality.

We recommended to the management to do the following courses of action:
The Municipal Accountant must immediately prepare the liquidation reports of
the total variance concerning the foregoing projects and submit them to COA for receipt (stamped) and signature by the auditor in the Reports of Checks Issued (RCI), if applicable, and Statement of Expenditures (SOE) before forwarding it to the Provincial Accountant.
Further, with fund transfers, reconciliation of the reciprocal accounts between the source agency and LGU Impasugong must be done regularly in order to assure uniformity of the balances and to insure correctness, reliability and accuracy of the financial statements. If necessary, the Municipal Accountant should often request a copy of subsidiary ledger from the source agency for reference.
Management must immediately submit the hard copies of the various Memorandum of Agreements (MOA) of the previously cited projects to the Office of the Auditor for review and verification and for reference in conducting more audit procedures.

We recommended to the management the following courses of action:
The Municipal Accountant should prepare an adjusting entry to correct the erroneous recognition of deferred income as presented below:
Other Deferred Credits			₱1,222,650.00
Prior Period Adjustment			₱1,222,650.00
The Municipal Treasurer should revisit the Development Agreement that the LGU previously entered into with Del Monte Philippines Inc.
The Municipal Treasurer should connect with Del Monte Philippines Inc. management for coordination and reconciliation of records related to the paid and unpaid rentals, if any.
The Municipal Treasurer should inquire and verify about the basis for the computation, if possible, of all the payments of rental fees made by Del Monte Philippines, Inc.
The Municipal Treasurer should trace back and review all the collections received by the LGU pertaining to the Development Agreement from December 2003 to December 2021 and compare them to the “should be collected rental fees” of the same period. If the result is insufficient payments of rental fees, the LGU of Impasugong must immediately demand payments thereof from Del Monte Philippines, Inc.
The computation of the “should be collection rental fees” must consider the 7% increase annually from the year 2005 to 2008 and from 2009 to 2021 must be based on the result of the reviewed assessment of the yearly rental.
The Municipal Treasurer should also check and verify whether Del Monte Philippines, Inc has paid the Goodwill bonus referred into the agreement. Otherwise, demand for the payments of the same thereof.
The Municipal Treasurer must prepare and maintain a subsidiary ledger exclusive for Del Monte Philippines Inc, to be used for the monitoring of rental and goodwill bonus related to the 78 hectares of agricultural land from the period January 2022 up to the end of the agreement on December 31, 2029.
Finally, furnish/update the Office of the Auditor of all the actions taken for the above-mentioned audit recommendations (a to h).

We recommended to the Municipal Engineer to discontinue the inclusion of costs of repairs and maintenance for motor vehicles in the Program of Works for the projects under the 20% development fund rather charge them directly to Maintenance and Other Operating Expenses (MOOE). And, for the subsequent periods, the Municipal Budget – OIC must review the Items of every Program of Work of every project or program under 20% Development Fund and must immediately disallow expenses of similar nature.  Management must strictly adhere to the guidelines on appropriation and utilization of the said fund in order to avoid disallowance in audit.

We recommended to the Bids and Awards Committee (BAC) Chairman to strictly adhere to Section 76 of the 2016 Implementing Rules and Regulations of RA 9184 for similar transactions in the future to prevent disallowance in audit.
We also recommended to the Municipal Budget In-charge to assure that the approved Appropriation Ordinance is available before issuing Certification of Funds Availability to ensure that the procurement is proper, valid and legal.
Moreover, the Municipal Accountant must include the new bank loan in the Notes to Financial Statements (NFTS) for the sake of public disclosure and transparency.

We recommended to the Technical Working Group (TWG) Head Secretariat to submit the copies of Sworn statement of the would-be recipient that they have been living together in the past 50 years of the 84 awardees (golden couples) to COA office for review and validation by the auditor. Non-submission of the required document may lead to the issuance of Notice of Suspension or Disallowance, if warranted.
Further, we recommended to the TWG Chairperson to submit a revised validation report with his and his members’ signatures in order to establish the correctness, validity and legality of the payment of cash awards amounted to ₱ 4,200,000.00.

We recommended to the management the following courses of action:
The Focal Person must conduct an ocular inspection immediately and verify if the project proposals were truly implemented. She must generate a detailed monitoring report, must indicate there-in the status and percentage of implementation substantiated with certifications from the head of the organization and colored photographs, and submit them to the Office of the Auditor for review and verification.
The Focal Person must coordinate with the Municipal Accountant in requiring for the Official Receipts (if possible) or Acknowledgment Receipts from the treasurers of all civil society organizations/ people’s organizations listed in Annex 9 who did not issue the same and submit them directly to the Office of the Auditor for confirmation. The treasurer of the organization must issue their own receipt in lieu of the Barangay’s unless the Barangay has direct involvement with the group.
The Focal Person must require Certificate of Registration from DOLE from all civil society organizations/ people’s organizations enumerated in Annex 9 which did not submit the same and furnish COA of the certified true copies for validation.
The LGU through the Municipal Treasurer must pay directly the forthcoming subsidies to the organization. The payee of the check must be the name of the civil society organization/people’s organization and not its Head or the Punong Barangay. This is an effective control or measure in order to avoid cash misappropriation, loss or embezzlement which may seriously affect the immediate realization of the proposed projects.
Submission of the foregoing required report and documents involving the disbursements of ₱ 1,340,000.00 is necessary in order to complete our post audit, otherwise, they will be subject to audit suspension or disallowance, if warranted.

We recommended to the management to immediately submit a legal basis and a written justification with a purpose or the like relative to the subsidies granted to school teachers, principals, national heads, etc. to COA Office in order to continue with our timely evaluation and post-audit.
We also recommended to the management to discontinue the release of similar subsidies or any expenses with similar nature such as donations, grants, contributions or gifts without clear and definite legal basis which can be considered irregular expenditures in the succeeding years to prevent disallowance in audit.

We recommended to the management the following courses of action:
To submit a clear and definite legal basis and a written justification for the payment of the monthly rice allowance and rice differential in CY 2021 which amounted to ₱ 2,636,000.00 and ₱ 4,323,750.00, respectively, to COA Office for further review and validation.
To refrain from paying its officials and employees with allowances, incentives and other fringe benefits not covered by existing and specific appropriations and have no clear and definite legal basis to prevent disallowance in audit as provided in COA Circular No. 2013-003 dated January 30, 2013.
To strictly observe the provision stipulated in DBM Budget Circular No. 2006-1 dated February 1, 2006 in the granting of rice allowance to LGU officials and personnel.

We recommended to the management to discontinue the practice of paying in advance the monthly allowances such as Representation Allowance and Transportation Allowance (RATA) and Personnel Economic Relief Allowance (PERA) including communication allowance, rice allowance and alimony to protect the interest of the government and to assure the propriety and validity of the grants.
Further, the Municipal Accountant should record all expenses on the period they are incurred to ascertain the fair presentation of the monthly financial statements.

We recommended to the Municipal Accountant the following:
To recognize and record the total project cost of the completed Philippine Rural Development Project which amounted to ₱ 55,399,368.17 immediately in the General Fund books of account under “Road Networks” to properly account the asset and to keep the fair presentation of the Statement of Financial Position. Also, to maintain a separate subsidiary ledger for the said asset.
To coordinate with the Municipal Engineer in securing the copy of the Certificate of Turnover from DA-PRDP as a documentary support of the Journal Entry Voucher (JEV) of the adjusting entry.

We recommended to the Municipal Accountant to immediately prepare an adjusting entry to transfer the cash variance lodged in the Due to Other Funds account to the General Fund in order for the cash account in the PRDP-Statement of Financial Position to be at par with the remaining cash balance presented in the Statement of Sources and Application of Funds (SSAF).

We recommended to the Municipal Mayor with the assistance of the Municipal Agriculturist to ensure that the milling machines be made available for use to its intended beneficiaries as soon as possible.
Further, the Municipal Agriculturist must strictly observe the Work and Financial Plan always in order to avoid delay in the implementation of the project and in providing benefits to the project beneficiaries.

We recommended to the Municipal Mayor to require the HRMO to implement the following courses of actions to establish the eligibility of the personnel granted with AHDP and SRA worth ₱ 1,512,560.55 as follows:
Require all the payees to submit their individual accomplishments reports signed by their supervisor(s) indicating that they directly catered to or were in contact with COVID-19 patients, persons under investigation, or persons under monitoring. The same should indicate their area of assignment;
Require the payees under check no. 1776886 to submit the unattached DTRs, and
Require the BHWs to submit copies of their certifications that they are indeed accredited and registered under the local health board.

We recommended to the management to first establish that the target recipients of incoming relief operations or feeding programs were severely affected by the COVID-19 pandemic situation by providing proofs such as local executive orders, declaration of localized lockdowns, certifications and the like before conducting the activities in order to ascertain that only qualified and affected residents receive the assistance.
Further, the LGU must see to it that they strictly adhere to the guidelines set forth in DILG-DBM JMC No. 01 dated March 27, 2020 on the proper utilization of the 20% Development fund to avoid disallowance in audit.

We recommended to the Municipal Accountant to book immediately the total value of the living farm animals which amounted to ₱ 1.206 million owned by the municipality supported with proper and complete documentations. Further, the Municipal Accountant must coordinate with the Municipal Agriculturist regularly as to the establishment of the total present value of every kind of living farm animal and adjust the said account accordingly if necessary. Furthermore, the Municipal Agriculturist must initiate to conduct the inventory count of these farm animals at the end of every semester and furnish the auditor and the Municipal Accountant directly with the final inventory report as valid reference for monitoring and recording of this government asset. The Municipal Agriculture Office must also report to COA immediately the duly documented death incidents of these farm animals for transparency and for checking and actual inspection by the assigned auditor.

We recommended to the Municipal Accountant to submit the required documentary supports of all the transactions abovementioned immediately to enable the auditor to continue with the validation of the year-end payables as to their existence, correctness and veracity, otherwise, make an adjusting entry and revert the same to surplus. We further recommended to the Municipal Accountant to insure that for the succeeding years Journal Entry Vouchers (JEVs) of accounts payable are duly supported with the required documentary requirements abovementioned. As an internal auditor of the agency, the required payable documents must be under her custody for safekeeping and must be readily available for review and verification upon request by the auditor to avoid suspension in audit.

We recommended to the BAC Chairman to submit the lacking documents enumerated in Annex A to COA immediately for further review and validation by the auditor and to justify the said claims. We recommended to the Human Resource Head to submit the written declarations of the responsible offices/delivery units in the completion of each critical service or other key processes to justify the rating of “Best Delivery Unit” and to rationalize the grant of ₱ 3.387 million worth of Performance-Based Bonus at a 0.65 multiple of the basic salary that was defrayed by the LGU to qualified government officials and personnel in December, 2020.

We recommended to the management to submit a legal basis, if possible, or a written justification of the said payment of 2019 Performance-Based Bonus amounted to ₱ 596,161.86. The LGU must refrain from paying irregular expenditures, at all instances, in order to avoid suspension and disallowance in audit.

We recommended to the Municipal Accountant to carefully review every accounting entry made before approving the Journal Entry Voucher (JEV) in order to avoid errors from occurring and in order to provide a more apt and accurate financial reports.

We recommended to the management to start procuring the POL requirements of the Municipality through competitive bidding in accordance with the provisions under Republic Act No. 9184 or the Government Procurement Law. Ideally, the LGU must provide its own tanks that will serve as containers for the procured bulk of petroleum products. If receptacles are not available, the LGU can negotiate with the winning bidder to deposit the procured dieseline and gasoline to its station and just draw from there when the need arises. Consequently, with their unstable and fluctuating prices and the contract amount as the limit, the quantity or the number of liters will certainly vary depending on the current price per liter in the market. In other words, more liters when the price per liter is discounted and lesser number of liters when the price is high. The daily utilization costs of the gas or diesel consigned by the LGU to the supplier will be consolidated until the total cost reach the original contract amount.

We recommended that the municipal accountant should:

Management promised to make the necessary adjustments and to stop charging items from the trust receipts generated from the sales of bid documents other than those payments for honoraria and overtime pay of personnel involved in the procurement process,

To avoid excessive and irregular payment of honoraria to personnel involve in procurement, we recommended the following:

That the Municipal Accountant should exercise prudence and diligence in the disbursement of funds and to strictly adhere to the provisions of payment of honoraria to BAC and TWG to avoid suspensions and disallowances in audit. She shall also ensure that savings from the approved local budget has been approved by the Sanggunian for payment of honoraria to BAC and TWG before effecting payment chargeable to the General Fund.
That the Municipal Budget Officer must refrain from allocating in the regular budget the honoraria of the BAC Chairman and its members. They must use first the collections from the sale of bidding documents and, in case of deficiency, use the agency savings with prior approval of the Sanggunian.

Management admitted their lapses during the Exit Conference and assured the audit team that they will stop charging honoraria of government personnel involved in the procurement process from the General Fund.

We recommended that the Accounting Office shall ensure that withholding taxes are deducted from payments made and the same are remitted to the BIR within the prescribed period. The accountant shall also consider the revised rate provided in R.A. 10963 or the Tax Reform for Acceleration & Inclusion (TRAIN) which is effective starting CY 2018.

The management, during the conduct of Exit conference, admitted that no taxes were withheld on payments of honoraria for Resource Speakers and the like. On the other hand, it was agreed that this will be implemented starting CY 2018.
The audit team also advised the management to send a letter to the Bureau of Internal Revenue for clarification on the interest rates in consideration of the adoption of R.A. 10963 otherwise known as the TRAIN Law.

The concerned officials should strictly adhere to the guidelines set forth by the DBM implementing the certain provisions of RA 7160 and refrain from paying honoraria to government officials other than those enumerated under DBM Compensation Policy Guideline No. 98-1.

The management pointed out during the Exit Conference that they already stopped paying the said honoraria right after they have received the AOM from the audit team. However, the Election Officer assigned in the Municipality requested for reconsideration by providing a COMELEC Resolution as his basis for the validity of his claim.

Recommendation:
We recommended that management strengthen the professionalization of the agency’s procuring unit.
Management promised to properly check the completeness of the supporting documents of contracts in accordance with the Revised IRR of RA 9184. However, they requested for leniency on contracts made in CY 2014 and prior years. The different officials composing the Bids and Awards Committee were already replaced and it would be difficult to trace previously requested documents.
The team reiterated that submission of incomplete supporting documents to COA will hinder auditorial and legal as well as indorsement to COA TITS for technical review.
The procurement activities of the LGU of Impasugong appeared to have lapses which is not in accordance with Rule I, Sections 2 and 3 of the Implementing Rules and Regulations (IRR) of the Republic Act (RA) No. 9184, thus, creating doubt as to transparency in its procurement process.
The audit team have conducted initial auditorial and legal review on contracts/purchase orders submitted by the agency since the year 2013 up to 2016. However, none was indorsed to COA Region 10 Technical Service Office for technical review due to lacking documents. In our initial auditorial and legal review, there were lacking documents to some of the contracts as shown in Annex C of this audit report.  As of the end of December 31, 2016, the lacking documents were not submitted to the Office of the Auditor despite demand.
Rule I, Section 3 of the IRR of RA 9184 states that: “x x x The provisions of this IRR are in line with the commitment of the GOP to promote good governance and its effort to adhere to the principle of transparency, accountability, equity, efficiency, and economy in the procurement process.  x x x”
Further, Rule I, Section 3 of the IRR of RA 9184 states that: “x x x the procurement of the GOP shall be governed by these principles: a) Transparency in the procurement process and in the implementation of procurement contracts x x x b) competitiveness by extending equal opportunity x x x e) public monitoring of the procurement process and the implementation of awarded contracts with the end in view of guaranteeing that these contracts are awarded pursuant to the provisions of the Act and this IRR, x x x “
This was discussed during the exit conferences in the years 2014, 2015 and 2016. It was mentioned that there was no BAC office designated wherein all the documents related to procurement activities can be kept in one place. This is one of the reason why some of the lacking documents cannot be submitted because the documents were not filed intact and nowhere to be located.
Another reason was the lack of thoroughness in checking the documents during the procurement process, such as but not limited to: undated documents, lack of signatures, wrong computation of NFCC.
Such observations created doubt as to transparency of its procurement activities. Also, liquidated damages cannot be determined, if any.
